coredata.dc:title: pH-responsive green fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for visualization of intracellular pH with intuitive color changes
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yuanyuan
Jiang, Tianyi
Zhong, Chuanqing
Zhao, Tingting
coredata.dc:description: Designing pH-sensitive nanoprobes for visualization of intracellular pH with intuitive color change is of high significance for better understanding the pathogenesis of diseases. However, most of the reported intracellular pH imaging strategies are the single intensity-based strategies, which are susceptible to interferences from background effects. In this work, pH-responsive green fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots were synthesized for visualization of intracellular pH with intuitive color change. The as-prepared green fluorescent N-CDs exhibited obvious red fluorescence when the pH value was less than 7, while only the green fluorescence intensity changed under neutral or alkaline conditions, which can be visually observed by the naked eye. In addition, the mechanism of the green fluorescence of the N-CDs was systematically investigated by density-functional theory (DFT) theoretical calculation together with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform-infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR), as well as the mechanism of the response of N-CDs to pH was systematically investigated by absorption spectrum and transmission electron microscope (TEM). Furthermore, the as-prepared green fluorescent N-CDs were used to visualize intracellular pH with intuitive color changes, demonstrating its promising potential in visual bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen doping
Mechanism investigation
Multicolor
Intracellular pH sensing
Visualization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Imaging of pH in living cells
Characterization of the green emissive N-CDs
Mechanism investigation of the green emissive N-CDs
Sensing of pH in aqueous solution and response mechanism investigation
Fluorescence visual imaging of intracellular pH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence excitation-emission map and (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (a) without and (b, c, d) with nitrogen dopping. The nitrogen doping amount is increasing from b to d. Dotted lines are the corresponding excitation spectra of the CDs.
High-resolution C1s, N1s and O1s XPS spectra for CDs (A) without and (B, C, D) with nitrogen dopping. The nitrogen doping amount is increasing from B to D.

(A) FT-IR spectra for CDs (a) without and (b, c, d) with nitrogen dopping. The nitrogen doping amount is increasing from b to d. (B) HOMO-LUMO gaps for the nitrogen doped systems. (C) Nitrogen doped models (AN1, AN2, AN3) and nitrogen free system AN0. Carbon (gray), hydrogen (white), nitrogen (red). (D) HOMO and LUMO calculated molecular orbitals for the nitrogen-doped systems.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs at different pH values. (B) Variation of excitation wavelengths, (C) variation of fluorescence intensity and (D) absorption spectrum of the N-CDs at different pH values.
TEM images of N-CDs at (A) pH = 2, (B) pH = 7, (C) pH = 9 and (D) pH = 12, respectively (The up and down inset shows the size distribution and HRTEM image, respectively).
Confocal images of HeLa cells treated with N-CDs (1/1000) at pH 3.0, 5.0, 7.0 and 9.0, respectively.
(A, B) Schematic of the synthesis of N-CDs and (C) its visual response to pH.

head.ce:title: pH-responsive green fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for visualization of intracellular pH with intuitive color changes

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: T. Zhao, CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, Gansu, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing pH-sensitive nanoprobes for visualization of intracellular pH with intuitive color change is of high significance for better understanding the pathogenesis of diseases. However, most of the reported intracellular pH imaging strategies are the single intensity-based strategies, which are susceptible to interferences from background effects. In this work, pH-responsive green fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots were synthesized for visualization of intracellular pH with intuitive color change. The as-prepared green fluorescent N-CDs exhibited obvious red fluorescence when the pH value was less than 7, while only the green fluorescence intensity changed under neutral or alkaline conditions, which can be visually observed by the naked eye. In addition, the mechanism of the green fluorescence of the N-CDs was systematically investigated by density-functional theory (DFT) theoretical calculation together with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform-infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR), as well as the mechanism of the response of N-CDs to pH was systematically investigated by absorption spectrum and transmission electron microscope (TEM). Furthermore, the as-prepared green fluorescent N-CDs were used to visualize intracellular pH with intuitive color changes, demonstrating its promising potential in visual bio-imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular pH is closely related with pathological and physiological processes, including cell metabolism, apoptosis, signal transduction and endocytosis [1,2]. Abnormal fluctuation of intracellular pH will result in serious diseases such as cancers, rheumatoid arthritis and other autophagy blockage and storage disorders [3]. Precise detection of intracellular pH can offer crucial information for cell functions and is of high significance for diseases diagnosis and prevention [4]. Hence, great efforts have been made to develop a series of cost-effective and sensitive approaches for real-time monitoring intracellular pH. Up to now, although numerous conventional organic fluorescent dyes or toxic semiconductor quantum dots based analytical techniques have been used for pH sensing [5–8], inevitable limitations such as insufficient photo-stability or complex synthesis still limit their application in providing accurate measured results. Therefore, developing new fluorescent materials with pH responsiveness, excellent optical stabilities and environmental compatibility is still of high significance for bio-sensing and bio-imaging applications.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), with unique advantages including high biocompatibility, low toxicity, low-cost synthesis process and excellent optical stabilities, have attracted enormous attentions in bio-sensing and bio-imaging applications [9–16]. Previous studies reported that N-CDs have been developed for accurate monitoring intracellular pH [17]. However, most reported N-CDs-based pH sensing strategies relied on single fluorescence intensity changes, the results of which may be interfered by the probe concentrations, environmental conditions and instrumental efficiency [18–20]. As alternatives, strategies that can visually detect intracellular pH based on fluorescence color changes rather than a single change in fluorescence intensity, could provide improved sensitivity by eliminating most of the ambiguities by self-calibration along with obvious color changes, which is benefit for rapid visual identification by the naked eye [21,22]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop N-CDs-based strategies for visual determination of intracellular pH according to fluorescence color changes.

Motivated by this, we synthesized pH-responsive N-CDs with green emission and applied them to the visual measurement and imaging of intracellular pH accompanied by fluorescence color changes (Scheme 1 ). Mechanism of the green fluorescence of the N-CDs caused by nitrogen doping was in-depth investigated through density functional theory (DFT) and experiments. Importantly, the fluorescence color of the prepared N-CDs changed from green to red at acidic pH, while only the fluorescence intensity changed under alkaline conditions, and the mechanism of such color change has also been preliminarily discussed. Furthermore, the prepared N-CDs was successfully applied for visual determination of pH fluctuations in living cells, indicating their great potential in practical bioanalysis.

1 g of hydroquinone was added to 10 mL of deionized water, followed by the addition of 0.6 mL of EDA, and then reacted at 180 °C without stirring for 10 h. Finally, by centrifuging the crude products at 12,000 rpm for 15 min and removing the larger aggregates, green emissive N-CDs were obtained.

Hela cells were seeded onto 96-well plates and grown for 24 h at 37 °C at 37 °C under 5 % CO2, and then treated with 10 μL of N-CDs (1/1000) for 24 h. Subsequently, 20 μL of MTT solution was added and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. Then, 200 μL of DMSO was added to each well and gently shaken for 10 min after removing the supernatants. At last, the absorbance values of the wells at 492 nm were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of pH in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Hela cells were treated with N-CDs (1/1000) for 2 h at 37 °C under 5 % CO2 atmosphere. Next, cells were washed with serum-free DMEM and then treated with buffer solutions of various pH (3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0) in the presence of KCl (125 × 10−3 M), NaCl (20 × 10−3 M), CaCl2 (0.5 × 10−3 M) and MgCl2 (0.5 × 10−3 M) for 20 min at 37 °C under 5 % CO2. Finally, confocal fluorescence images were obtained using confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the green emissive N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The green emissive N-CDs with an absolute quantum yield of 28.6 % were synthesized by using hydroquinone as carbon source and EDA as nitrogen doping precursors. As shown in Fig. 1A, with the increase of nitrogen doping amount, the optimal excitation/emission field of CDs gradually redshifts (excitation from 310 nm to 405 nm; emission from 360 nm to 510 nm), while the excitation/emission wavelength is no longer redshifted when the nitrogen doping amount reaches saturation. The UV–visible absorption spectra in Fig. 1B indicates that three similar absorption peaks appeared in the high energy region at 260 nm with the increase of nitrogen doping amount as well as three different absorption peaks also appeared in the low energy region at 395 nm, 405 nm and 406 nm, respectively. It is worth noting that the absorption peaks in the low energy region gradually redshifted with the increase of nitrogen doping amount and no longer redshifted when nitrogen doping amount reaches saturation, which may be due to the difference of surface states of CDs caused by the increasing nitrogen doping, resulting in different n-π* transitions [23]. Since nitrogen doping amount reaches saturation, the surface state of CDs does not change and the absorption peaks no longer redshift, which is consistent with previous report [24]. In addition, the absorption peaks in the low energy region is close to the corresponding optimal excitation wavelength, indicating that the fluorescence emission of N-CDs originates from the absorption in the low energy region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism investigation of the green emissive N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the mechanism of the redshift for the green fluorescence of the N-CDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, the size of CDs does not show significant changes with the increase of nitrogen doping amount, indicating that the fluorescence emission redshift is independent of size effect. It is reported that nitrogen doping (especially amino nitrogen) and enhanced surface oxidation degree are beneficial for reducing the band gap of LUMO-HOMO and leading to fluorescence redshift of CDs [25–27]. The high-resolution N1s and O1s XPS spectra shown in Fig. 2 indicated that the peak at 399 eV and 530.2 eV for amino nitrogen and CO gradually increased with the increasing nitrogen doping amount, respectively, and no longer increased when nitrogen doping amount reaches saturation.

In addition, the FT-IR analysis shown in Fig. 3A indicated that the intensity of the characteristic stretching vibration peak for O-H/N-H at 3140–3490 cm−1 and bending vibration peak for NH at 1520 cm−1 as well as stretching vibration peak for CO at 1670 cm−1 also gradually increased with the increasing nitrogen doping amount, respectively, and no longer increased when nitrogen doping amount reaches saturation. The results of FT-IR and XPS analysis both demonstrated that the increasing nitrogen doping (especially amino nitrogen) and enhanced surface oxidation degree contribute to the fluorescence redshift of CDs.

Moreover, DFT calculation was carried out for in-depth investigation of the influence of amino nitrogen on the fluorescence redshift of CDs. According to the doping amount of amino nitrogen, four models named AN0, AN1, AN2 and AN3 were established (Fig. 3C), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3B, compared with the no nitrogen doped system AN0, the reduced HOMO-LUMO gap can be observed with the increasing doping amount of amino nitrogen from AN0 to AN3 (Fig. 3D shows the corresponding HOMO and LUMO calculated molecular orbitals), the result of which further indicated that the introduction of amino nitrogen into the carbon lattice could lead to the reduced HOMO-LUMO gaps, resulting in the fluorescence redshift of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of pH in aqueous solution and response mechanism investigation
ce:section.ce:para: The response of N-CDs to pH in aqueous solution was subsequently investigated. As shown in Fig. 4A, green fluorescence changed to red fluorescence when the pH value was less than 7, and the red fluorescence intensity gradually enhanced along with the decreased pH value. When the pH values ranged from 7 to 9, the green fluorescence of N-CDs gradually increases, while the fluorescence weakened when the pH value is greater than 10, and the stronger the alkalinity, the weaker the fluorescence. In addition, when the pH value was less than 7, the optimal emission wavelength was obviously redshifted from 510 nm to 600 nm (Fig. 4B), while the optimal excitation wavelength was redshifted from 405 nm to 482 nm (Fig. 4C). When pH value is greater than 7, no obvious changes for the optimal emission and excitation wavelength was observed. Moreover, applications of visual detecting pH in water was also performed using fluorescent test paper prepared by printing N-CDs onto filter paper under UV light. As shown in Fig. S2, when the pH value is less than 7, the test paper shows obvious red fluorescence colors, and the red fluorescence gradually increases as the pH value decreases. Under weak alkalinity (pH: 7–9), the green fluorescence gradually increased, and under strong alkalinity (pH: 10–14), the green fluorescence gradually decreased. That is to say, we can roughly determine the pH value of the water with the naked eye according to the fluorescent color of the test paper.

In view of the response of N-CDs to pH, absorption spectrum of the N-CDs at different pH values were firstly measured for the mechanism investigation. As shown in Fig. 4D, when pH is less than 7, a new absorption peak appeared in the low energy region and gradually redshifted with increasing acidity, indicating that new substances may be formed under acidic conditions and lead to reduced LUMO-HOMO band gap of N-CDs [28]. When in weak alkalinity (pH from 7 to 9), only the absorption intensity gradually increased and no new absorption peaks appeared. When in strong alkalinity (pH greater than 12), the absorption peak of N-CDs almost disappeared, indicating the disappearance of N-CDs. The above results of absorption spectra were consistent with the changes of fluorescence color and intensity of N-CDs at different pH.

In order to further investigate the mechanism of the response of N-CDs to pH, TEM images of N-CDs at different pH values (taking pH = 2, 7, 9 and 12 as examples) were obtained. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, the N-CDs obviously aggregated into larger particles when pH = 2, which is easy to lose the quantum confinement effect, resulting in the reduced LUMO-HOMO band gap and fluorescence redshift of N-CDs [29]. In addition, dynamic light scattering (DLS) results shown in Fig. S3 also confirms the aggregation of N-CDs at pH = 2. When pH ranged from 7 to 9, there was no significant change in the particle size of N-CDs (Fig. 5C). It is reported that a “protective shell” with negative charges will be formed when the carboxyl group on the surface of N-CDs is deprotonated, thus reducing the non-radiative decay and contributing to fluorescence enhancement. [30] When in strong alkalinity (pH = 12), N-CDs will be decomposed (Fig. 5D), resulting in decreased fluorescence. Here, the absorption peaks of the N-CDs in the low-energy region is corresponding to the n–π* transition of CN, C–N–C or CO structures. It is worth noting that in acidic or alkaline environment, the surface state of N-CDs will change, resulting in a double peak in the low energy region [31,32]. However, in the strong alkaline environment, the N-CDs will decompose and the absorption peak will disappear, which is also consistent with the TEM results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence visual imaging of intracellular pH
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to visual imaging of intracellular pH, MTT assay was carried out to investigate the cytotoxicity of the N-CDs and showed their low cytotoxicity (Fig. S4). Subsequently, the performance of the N-CDs for visual imaging of intracellular pH was confirmed. Hela cells were firstly treated with N-CDs (1/1000) for 2 h and then incubated with buffer solutions of various pH (3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0) for another 20 min. As shown in Fig. 6 , Compared with green fluorescence in neutral or weakly alkaline environments, red fluorescence can be obviously observed in acidic environments. These results successfully demonstrated that the N-CDs can serve as a promising probe for visual imaging of intracellular pH based on color changes.

In summary, we synthesized pH-responsive green fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots and applied them for visualization of intracellular pH with intuitive color changes. DFT theoretical calculation together with XPS and FT-IR spectrum clearly demonstrated that enhanced surface oxidation degree and reduced band gap of LUMO-HOMO resulted from amino nitrogen doping mainly contributes to the green fluorescence of the N-CDs. The as-prepared green fluorescent N-CDs exhibited obvious red fluorescence when the pH value was less than 7, while only the green fluorescence intensity changed under neutral or alkaline conditions. Absorption spectrum and TEM were also performed to investigated the mechanism of the response of N-CDs to pH. Most importantly, visualization of intracellular pH with intuitive color changes can be achieved, indicating its promising potential in visual bio-imaging. We expect that this work will expand the study of luminescence mechanism of N-CDs and provide a promising avenue for visual biological imaging.
Yuanyuan Liu: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Tianyi Jiang: Data curation. Chuanqing Zhong: Methodology. Tingting Zhao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge support from the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2021MB099) and the Doctoral Fund of Shandong Jianzhu University (X21021Z).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110496.
